title: Kaworus I love you
wikitext:
[]: Kaworu's "I love you"             Quote Post by chaosakita » Wed Nov 14, 2012 10:43 pm When Kaowru says "I love you" to Shinji in episode 24, he uses the Japanese word "koi" right? But isn't that word usually associated with romance? And if so, can anyone explain that to me? I'd like to check, but youtube took all the Japanese audio versions of 24 down. :(

[]:             Quote Post by riffraff11235 » Wed Nov 14, 2012 11:25 pm If we just cut it down to the bit of dialogue in question: Episode 24 wrote: Kaworu - "Sou. Koi ni atai suru yo." = "Yes. That earns my sympathy ." Shinji - "Koi?" = "Sympathy?" Kaworu - " Suki tte koto sa." = "In other words, I love you ."

[]:             Quote Post by chaosakita » Wed Nov 14, 2012 11:39 pm Oh that explains it. Like I said I don't have the dialogue available with me. I'm pretty sure now that koi only refers to romance then.

[]:             Quote Post by Hyper Shinchan » Thu Nov 15, 2012 12:18 am Kaworu says 好意/kōi , or "koui" if you prefer this form instead of the circumflex o, not 恋/koi; I wonder if Japanese speakers can exchange one with the other though, I get a pair of results for ""恋に値するよ"" but I don't understand the context.

[]:             Quote Post by Rj123541 » Thu Nov 15, 2012 2:29 pm 好意/kōi means "kindness" and 恋に値するよ means "worthy of love". Now having said that, it has no romantic meaning to my knowledge.

[]:             Quote Post by JoeD80 » Thu Nov 15, 2012 5:25 pm riffraff11235 wrote: "Suki" is the word we need to look at. From what I've seen, it can be used with either romantic or platonic meaning.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Fri Nov 16, 2012 4:17 pm Most of the love confessions in all sorts of anime involve "suki", "aishite" is probably considered too strong a word for teenagers or stuff. With the blush and the hand-holding, the context is hard to miss - heck, even without the romantic context, I'd say "I love you" fits it better, if only in a greek-philosopher-way - That's just how Kaworu expresses himself, the tendency of his employers for big philosophic talk must've rubbed off on him.
summary:
